issues: color_temperature_mismatch: candidate axe blade is distinctly orange/rust-warm vs peer weapons which are silver/steel or gold — not a standard palette member
highlight_intensity: orange blade face is more saturated and higher-contrast than the muted metallic sheen on peer weapons

scale_disparity: candidate renders noticeably larger than all peer sprites in the reference sheet, undermining the 'matching scale' claim and suggesting proportion normalization was not done rigorously

charm_level: peer helmets carry character detail (horns, crests, visor slits) giving them personality; candidate axe is geometrically competent but visually inert — below the warmth/charm register of the family
palette_overlap: only minimal palette intersection — gray handle partially overlaps with peer metals, but the dominant orange-rust hue is absent from all peer items visible in the sheet
reasoning: Rendering style (hard-edge pixel art, 1px black outline) and shadow direction (top-left source) align with the peer set. However, the candidate departs on several style consistency axes that matter: (1) The axe blade's dominant hue is a warm orange-rust, whereas every peer weapon in the sheet reads either cool silver-gray or warm gold-yellow — orange occupies a gap between those two families and pulls the candidate out of palette. (2) The blade's lit face is more vividly saturated than peer highlights, which are restrained to a single lighter tone rather than a full orange ramp. (3) Physical size in the composite image is roughly 3-4× any single peer sprite; even if the item is canonically larger, the composite should normalise to a common canvas size — this was not done, making cross-comparison unreliable and suggesting pipeline slippage. (4) Charm register is below par: peers carry deliberate design charm (Viking horns, Roman plumes), while the candidate is a plain utilitarian silhouette with no character signal. Passing on rendering technique but failing on palette coherence and charm target.
